content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

March 2, 1994

Robert E. Leidigh
Olson, Hagel, Fong, Leidigh,
Waters, and Fishburn
300 Capitol Mall, Suite 350
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-94-060

Dear Mr. Leidigh:

This is in response to your letter requesting reconsideration
of a portion of the informal advice letter¹ provided to you on
December 7, 1993 (Advice Letter No. I-93-439), on behalf of the
Senate Rules Committee regarding application of Section 89001 of
the Political Reform Act (the "Act").²

In the prior advice letter, we reviewed several samples of
mailings that you wished to distribute. Each consisted of: (1) a
personalized cover letter from the senator which will be signed by
the senator, and (2) an informational pamphlet which will include
a photograph of the senator and the senator's name and office
address.

You have requested reconsideration of our conclusion that
three of the sample letters (dealing with crime and personal
safety) were considered substantially similar and needed to be

_____________

¹ Please note that informal assistance does not provide the
requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal
written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

² Government Code Section 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations, Sections
18000-18954. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Our File No. I-94-060
Page 2


aggregated for purposes of the mass mailing prohibition in Section
89001 of the Act.  Specifically, the three letters were the
following:

     SAMPLE D: The cover letter discusses the cause and
occurrence of rape and states that there are ways to minimize the
occurrence of rape.  The letter then describes the pamphlet and
restates tips which are included in the informational brochure.
The letter will be signed by the senator.

     The pamphlet (Preventing Sexual Assault) provides facts about
rape, prevention tips in the home, on the street and in a car, and
also what to do after an assault occurs.  The senator's name will
appear on the front of the pamphlet under the phrase "compliments
of" and on the back will appear a photograph of the senator and
the senator's name and office address.

     SAMPLE E: The cover letter discusses heightened awareness of
crime because of media coverage and states that there are ways to
avoid being a victim.  The letter then describes the pamphlet and
restates tips which are included in the informational brochure.
The letter will be signed by the senator.

     The pamphlet (Crime Prevention Tips for Seniors) discusses
home security, and safety in the streets and in a car.  The
pamphlet also discusses what to do if someone is a victim in their
own home.  The senator's name will appear on the front of the
pamphlet under the phrase "compliments of" and on the back will
appear a photograph of the senator and the senator's name and
office address.

     SAMPLE F: The cover letter begins with the same paragraph as
the prior letter, discussing heightened awareness of crime because
of media coverage and states that persons need to be alert.  The
letter then describes the pamphlet and states that it will help in
avoiding crime.

     The pamphlet (Personal Security) safety in the streets and in
cars, on buses and at the office.  The pamphlet also discusses
what to do if a crime occurs.  The senator's name will appear on
the front of the pamphlet under the phrase "compliments of" and on
the back will appear a photograph of the senator and the senator's
name and office address.

     You have asked us to reconsider our conclusion with respect
to Sample D.  You have not asked for reconsideration of the
conclusion with respect to Sample E and F, but only as they relate
to Sample D.

                              DISCUSSION

     Regulation 18901 applies only to a "mass mailing" as defined
in the Act.  "Mass mailing" means over 200 substantially similar

Our File No. I-94-060
Page 3


pieces of mail. (Section 82041.5; Regulation 18901(a)(4).) If
less than 201 substantially similar items are sent in a calendar
month, the regulation would not restrict the mailing.

"Substantially similar" is defined in Regulation 18901(c)(3)
as follows:

(A)  Two items are "substantially similar" if
any of the following applies:

1. The items are identical, except for
changes necessary to identify the recipient
and his or her address.

2. The items are intended to honor,
commend, congratulate, or recognize an
individual or group, or individuals or groups,
for the same event or occasion; are intended
to celebrate or recognize the same holiday; or
are intended to congratulate an individual or
group, or individuals or groups, on the same
type of event, such as birthdays or an-
niversaries.

3. Both of the following apply to the
items mailed:

a. Most of the bills, legislation,
governmental action, activities, events,
or issues of public concern mentioned in
one item are mentioned in the other.

b. Most of the information
contained in one item is contained in the
other.

(B) Enclosure of the same informational
materials in two items mailed, such as copies of
the same bill, public document, or report, shall
not, by itself, mean that the two items are
"substantially similar." Such informational
materials may not include the elected officer's
name, photograph, signature, or any other reference
to the elected officer except as permitted
elsewhere in this regulation.

Issues of Public Concern

In our prior letter, we concluded that the letters discuss
the same issue of public concern. The three for which you
requested advice all deal with crime and victims. We stated that
while "various aspects of crime are focused on, it would appear
that the 'issue of public concern' in each is crime."

Our File No. I-94-060
Page 4


Most of the Information is Identical

     We also concluded that the samples in question (D, E, and F)
would be considered substantially similar since each letter
included shared language, and in addition, many of the tips
provided in the attachments were identical.

     Upon further review, we have reached a different conclusion
with respect to Sample D concerning rape. While much of the
information in D is also contained in E and F, we do not believe
that "most" of this information is identical. Thus, Sample D
would be considered a separate item. This same conclusion would
apply to the brochure accompanying Sample D.

     If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.3

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Steven G. Churchwell
                                        General Counsel


                                        [illegible]
                                        By: John W. Wallace
                                            Counsel, Legal Division




___________________________
3    Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries. Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 10¢ per page.
